content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 4, 1991

Ms. Diane M. Fishburn
OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL,
FONG & LEIDIGH
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. I-91-397

Dear Ms. Fishburn:

We have received your letter dated August 16, 1991, in which
you seek advice on behalf of Carmen Sabatino regarding the "sender
identification" provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

As Mr. Sabatino's authorized representative we provide you
with advice pursuant to Regulation 18329, but as your request does
not reference a pending governmental decision, the advice provided
is informal only.² The following advice is based upon the facts
provided in your letter of August 16, 1991, and supplemented by
telephone conversations with your office on August 22, August 28,
and October 1, 1991.

QUESTION

Is Mr. Sabatino's publication, "Check and Balance," subject
to the Act's "sender identification" requirements?

CONCLUSION

As a mass mailing sent by a candidate or a committee, the
publication "Check and Balance" is subject to the sender
identification requirements of the Act.

_________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seg. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-91-397
Page 2

FACTS

Carmen Sabatino is a candidate for the office of mayor for
the City of Modesto in the November 1991 election. Mr. Sabatino
founded Carino Publications which publishes "Check and
Balance" on a monthly or bimonthly basis. You have indicated that
the publication addresses local issues, government news, and
elections, and provides Mr. Sabatino with a forum in which to
editorialize on these topics.

Check and Balance has a paid subscription list of between 300
and 500, is distributed free of charge to individuals and groups,
and is available for purchase at two local bookstores (recently,
35 copies were sold at the bookstores during a one month period).
You have indicated that Mr. Sabatino expects to include
information relating to the forthcoming municipal election,
including endorsing his own candidacy and discussing the
candidacies of others for other municipal offices. We note that
when Mr. Sabatino unsuccessfully ran for Assembly in 1990, Check
and Balance prominently featured his candidacy and urged readers
to support him in the election.

Finally, you have indicated that Check and Balance, formed in
1988, is intended to be an ongoing enterprise, continuing after
the November, 1991 election, and that there are presently no plans
to alter the publication's format or distribution. You have also
indicated that Mr. Sabatino does not use funds of his campaign
committee to publish or distribute Check and Balance.

ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 84305 provides that a candidate or
committee which sends a mass mailing must include the "name,
street address, and city of the candidate or committee on the
outside of each piece of mail in the mass mailing and on at least
one of the inserts...." (Section 84305(a).) When the sender of
the mass mailing is a single candidate or committee, the
identification of the name, street address, and city of the
candidate or committee is required only on the outside of each
piece of mail. (Section 84305(b).) When the sender of a mass
mailing is a controlled committee, the name of the person
controlling the committee is to be included in addition to the
information required by subdivision (a).

A "mass mailing" is over two hundred substantially similar
pieces of mail sent in a calendar month. (Section 82041.5.) The
"sender" of a mass mailing is defined as the candidate or
committee paying for the largest portion of reportable
expenditures attributed to the design, printing, and posting of
the mailing. (Regulation 18435(b).)

File No. I-91-397
Page 3


Regulation 18225

You have suggested that Check and Balance falls within the
"editorial exception" of Regulation 18225(b)(4)(A), thus exempting
the publication from the sender identification requirement, and
have referred to the Commission's opinion in In Re Christiansen
(Goleta Valley Today), 1 FPPC Ops. 170 (1975), to support this
suggestion.

An exception to the definition of "expenditure" applies to
those costs incurred by a "regularly published newspaper, magazine
or other periodical of general circulation" routinely providing
news, articles, and general interest commentary. (Regulation
18225(b)(4)(A).)

Check and Balance, because it is published and distributed by
a candidate for office, does not qualify for this exception.
Regulation 18225(a)(2) provides that an expenditure is a payment
made by a candidate unless it is clear that the payment was made
for personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status
as an officeholder.

The definition of "expenditure" in Regulation 18225(b) and
the exceptions to the definition as provided in subsection (b)(4)
specifically exclude expenditures made by candidates. Regulation
18225(b) states that the definition of expenditure is applicable
to a payment made "by any person, other than those persons . . .
described in subsection (a)..." Candidates are included in
subsection (a). Therefore, the "editorial exception" as provided
for in subsection (b)(4)(A) is not applicable to Check and Balance
because the expenditures made for the publication are made by a
candidate.

When Mr. Sabatino makes a payment/expenditure for a purpose
related to his candidacy, the payment or expenditure is made for a
political purpose. (Regulation 18225(a).) In previously
responding to a candidate who produced and distributed his own
newspaper, the Commission has indicated that when the publication
was used as a means of supporting his candidacy, the costs
incurred in connection with its publication and distribution were
reportable and discloseable. (Warren Advice Letter, A-86-120.)
Because exceptions as provided by subsection (b) are not
applicable, this would also appear to be the case with respect to
Check and Balance. The sender identification requirements of
Section 84305 would therefore be applicable to Mr. Sabatino's
publication at least subsequent to the time when he is a candidate
within the meaning of Section 82007.³

_________________________________________________________________

³ Under rule ennunciated in Regulation 18531.5, because Mr.
Sabatino directs and controls the decisions of his campaign
committee and Carino Publications, the committee and Carino
Publications are considered one entity for purposes of
expenditures that support his candidacy or support or oppose other
candidates for elective office or positions on ballot measures.

File No. I-91-397
Page 4


First Amendment Considerations

     Section 84305 does not prohibit Mr. Sabatino from publishing
his newsletter nor interfere with his ability to express his views
on an election campaign. Section 84305 only requires a specific
sender identification that Mr. Sabatino has already essentially
provided elsewhere in the publication itself.

     Your reference to Schuster v. Municipal Court (1980) 109
Cal.App.3d 887) does not change this conclusion. In Schuster, the
appellate court invalidated former Elections Code Section 29410,
which in part provided that

          (a) Every person ... is guilty of a
          misdemeanor who causes to be reproduced ...
          any ... matter having reference to an
          election, to any candidate, or to any measure,
          or causes such reproduced matter to be posted
          or distributed, unless there appears ... the
          name and address of the business or residence
          of a person responsible for it.

     The court invalidated this section as an overbroad
infringement of the constitutional right to distribute anonymous
literature. (Schuster, supra, 109 Cal.App.3d at 894.) The basis
for this holding, however, was that the statute scrutinized by the
court prohibited all anonymous literature of any kind. (Schuster,
supra, 109 Cal.App.3d at 891.) The application of Section 84305
is not comparable in scope to the statute invalidated in Schuster.

     Finally, we wish to note that in the copies of Check and
Balance that you have provided, Mr. Sabatino prominently discloses
his name as the publisher, as well as the name and address of the
publishing company. As such, Check and Balance is not the type of
"anonymous publication" at issue in Schuster that we will, as you
suggest, somehow be silenced if the sender identification
requirements of Section 84305 are imposed. The necessary
information is already being provided within the publication.

     All that is required by Section 84305 is that the name,
street address, and city of the candidate or committee sending the
mass mailing be placed on the outside of each piece of mail in the
mass mailing, and perhaps on at least one of the inserts. When
the sender of a mass mailing is a controlled committee, the name
of the person controlling the committee is also included. In
other words, information that Mr. Sabatino already provides in the
publication must be provided one additional time on the outside of
the piece of mail.

File No. I-91-397
Page 5


     Finally, we believe you mischaracterize the sender
identification as requiring identification "as campaign
literature." Application of Section 84305 does not create a
campaign literature. Under the facts as you have presented them,
we believe the recipients of Check and Balance who conclude that
the publication is campaign literature do so because of the
content of the publication, and not because of Section 84305.

     I hope this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. Please contact me if you have any further questions
regarding this matter at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jonathan S. Rothman
                                            Counsel, Legal Division
